function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Using Q1 2026 AI capex and annualized AI revenue, estimate an implied capex-to-sales multiple. Compare this with Connectivity and assess what the disparity implies regarding maturity versus infrastructure-build phase economics.

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the company's structure, segments, and financials. I need to find:

1. AI segment capex (Q1 2026)
2. AI segment revenue (annualized from Q1 2026)
3. Connectivity segment capex and revenue for comparison

Let me begin by parsing the S-1 filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve information about the segments, AI capex, AI revenue, Connectivity capex, and Connectivity revenue for Q1 2026.

arguments.prompt: What are the operating segments disclosed in this S-1 filing? List all segments and describe what each one does. {{spacex_s1}}
What is the AI segment capex for Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026)? Also, what is AI revenue for Q1 2026? {{spacex_s1}}
What is the Connectivity segment capex for Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026)? Also, what is Connectivity revenue for Q1 2026? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpts, the company discloses **three operating and reportable segments**: Space, Connectivity, and AI. Below is a list of each segment and a description of its business activities as stated in the filing.

---

### 1. Space Segment
*“In our Space segment, we design, manufacture, and launch reusable rockets to provide high cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale.”*  
*(Also described in the financial statement notes as: “designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets to provide high cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space at unprecedented scale”)*

The Space segment serves as the foundational competitive advantage for the company, offering launch services, satellite-deployment capabilities, and space‑based technology to government, commercial, and other customers. It includes the Falcon launch vehicles, Dragon spacecraft, and the development of the fully reusable Starship system.

---

### 2. Connectivity Segment
*“In our Connectivity segment, we operate a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband data and communications network powered by over 9,600 Starlink broadband and mobile satellites in Low‑Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity to millions of consumer, enterprise, and government customers across 164 countries, territories, and other markets.”*  
*(Also noted as: “operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low‑Earth Orbit, delivering connectivity … through our Starlink offering”)*

The Connectivity segment is built around the Starlink satellite constellation, providing broadband internet services globally, including to remote and underserved areas. It includes Starlink Consumer Broadband, enterprise solutions, and satellite‑to‑mobile (Starlink Mobile) voice and texting services.

---

### 3. AI Segment
*“In our AI segment, we operate a highly vertically integrated AI platform spanning our truth‑seeking frontier model Grok, AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, X—our real‑time information, entertainment, and free speech platform—and AI computational infrastructure.”*  
*(Also: “operates a vertically integrated AI platform spanning a frontier LLM Grok, AI solutions for consumer and enterprise customers, X — a real‑time information, entertainment, and free speech platform — and AI computational infrastructure.”)*

The AI segment encompasses:
- **Grok** – a frontier large language model (LLM) and consumer/enterprise AI assistant (including features such as Grok Voice, Grok Imagine, and Grok Teams).
- **X platform** – a real‑time information, entertainment, and free‑speech platform that integrates AI tools.
- **AI compute infrastructure** – the vertically built data‑center and computational resources that power both Grok and the AI‑enabled features of X.

---

All three segments operate as a vertically integrated innovation engine, with the Connectivity and AI segments expected to be the primary drivers of near‑term revenue growth.

Based on the provided financial sections, here is the information for Q1 2026:

**AI segment capex for Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026):** $7,723 million

**AI revenue for Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026):** $818 million

Based on the provided document snippets, here are the figures for the Connectivity segment for Q1 2026 (three months ended March 31, 2026):

*   **Connectivity segment capex:** $1,332 million
*   **Connectivity revenue:** $3,257 million

steps.reasoning_content:
Excellent. Let me now compute the implied capex-to-sales multiples.

For AI segment:
- Q1 2026 capex: $7,723 million
- Q1 2026 revenue: $818 million
- Annualized AI revenue: $818 million × 4 = $3,272 million
- Capex-to-sales ratio (using annualized revenue): $7,723 / $3,272 = ?

For Connectivity segment:
- Q1 2026 capex: $1,332 million
- Q1 2026 revenue: $3,257 million
- Annualized Connectivity revenue: $3,257 million × 4 = $13,028 million
- Capex-to-sales ratio (using annualized revenue): $1,332 / $13,028 = ?

Wait, let me think about this differently. The question says "implied capex-to-sales multiple". Typically this could mean:
- Quarterly capex divided by annualized quarterly revenue
- Or it could be something else

Let me compute both interpretations. But first, let me also look at what other financial details might be important - like segment-level profitability, capex as percentage of revenue, etc.

Let me also check if there's a distinction between capex and capital expenditures in the filing, and whether there are more detailed segment financials.

Let me also get more context about the AI and Connectivity segments from the MD&A or other parts of the filing.

arguments.prompt: What are the detailed segment financials including revenue, operating income/loss, and capital expenditures for AI and Connectivity segments for Q1 2026 and any prior periods? Show the full segment disclosure table. {{spacex_s1}}
What is the definition of capital expenditures (capex) as used in this filing? How is capex defined and calculated for each segment? {{spacex_s1}}

**Key AI and Connectivity highlights from the tables:**
- **Connectivity** – Q1 2026 revenue $3,257M, operating income $1,188M, capex $1,332M; full-year 2025 revenue $11,387M, operating income $4,423M, capex $4,178M.
- **AI** – Q1 2026 revenue $818M, operating loss $(2,469)M, capex $7,723M; full-year 2025 revenue $3,201M, operating loss $(6,355)M, capex $12,727M.

Based on the provided text, the filing does not contain a single, formal sentence that begins with "Capital expenditures are defined as...". Instead, the definition must be inferred from how the expenditures are described in the Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) and the cash flow statement.

**Definition of Capital Expenditures (Capex)**
Capital expenditures in this filing are primarily defined by their nature as **purchases of property, plant, and equipment (PP&E)**. This is directly evidenced in the cash flow statement line item: “Purchases of property, plant, and equipment,” which ties to the total capex figures.

The MD&A describes capex as investments in long-term physical assets, including construction, infrastructure, and equipment that has not yet been placed in service (construction-in-progress), such as "the build out of data centers and related infrastructure, and space launch facilities and related infrastructure."

**How Capex is Defined and Calculated for Each Segment**
The filing does not provide a specific calculation formula or a definition that varies by segment. Instead, it describes what the expenditures consist of for each segment and presents the total amount for each.

*   **Space:** Capex is driven by **investments in launch site infrastructure**, specifically for the Starship program and other launch facilities. Additionally, impairment charges for damaged flight vehicles are recorded in the Space segment.
*   **Connectivity:** Capex consists of **satellite and ground equipment costs** related to expanding the satellite network.
*   **AI:** Capex is driven by **investments in terrestrial data centers**, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure. A portion of this is classified as “AI infrastructure” within construction-in-progress.

Calculation-wise, the filing presents these as simple, aggregated dollar figures for each segment without showing any underlying allocations or formulas. The line item “Total Capital Expenditures” is the sum of the three segment figures.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compute the capex-to-sales multiples.

For the AI segment (Q1 2026):
- Capex: $7,723 million
- Revenue: $818 million
- Annualized revenue: $818 × 4 = $3,272 million
- Capex-to-sales (using annualized revenue): $7,723 / $3,272 = ?

Let me compute this:
7,723 / 3,272 = 2.3604... So about 2.36x

For the Connectivity segment (Q1 2026):
- Capex: $1,332 million
- Revenue: $3,257 million
- Annualized revenue: $3,257 × 4 = $13,028 million
- Capex-to-sales (using annualized revenue): $1,332 / $13,028 = ?

1,332 / 13,028 = 0.1022... So about 0.10x

Now let me also look at this on a quarterly (non-annualized) basis for comparison:
- AI: 7,723 / 818 = 9.44x quarterly
- Connectivity: 1,332 / 3,257 = 0.41x quarterly

The question specifically says "annualized AI revenue" so I should use the annualized approach.

Let me also look at trends to support analysis. Let me also compute some additional ratios.

AI segment:
- Capex-to-revenue (annualized): 7,723 / (818 × 4) = 7,723 / 3,272 = 2.36x
- Operating margin: (2,469) / 818 = -301.8%
- D&A as % of revenue: 1,493 / 818 = 182.5%

Connectivity segment:
- Capex-to-revenue (annualized): 1,332 / (3,257 × 4) = 1,332 / 13,028 = 0.102x
- Operating margin: 1,188 / 3,257 = 36.5%
- D&A as % of revenue: 783 / 3,257 = 24.0%

The disparity is enormous: AI capex is 2.36x annualized revenue while Connectivity capex is only 0.10x annualized revenue.

This implies:
- AI is in a heavy infrastructure-build phase, spending massively ahead of revenue generation
- Connectivity has matured to a point where capex is a small fraction of revenue, suggesting infrastructure already built and generating substantial returns

Let me now also look at historical trends for both segments to add more depth.

Let me compute year-over-year trends:

AI:
- FY2023: capex $463M, revenue $2,961M → capex/sales = 0.16x
- FY2024: capex $5,633M, revenue $2,620M → capex/sales = 2.15x
- FY2025: capex $12,727M, revenue $3,201M → capex/sales = 3.98x
- Q1 2026 annualized: capex $7,723M, revenue $3,272M → capex/sales = 2.36x

Connectivity:
- FY2023: capex $2,455M, revenue $3,869M → capex/sales = 0.63x
- FY2024: capex $3,498M, revenue $7,599M → capex/sales = 0.46x
- FY2025: capex $4,178M, revenue $11,387M → capex/sales = 0.37x
- Q1 2026 annualized: capex $1,332M, revenue $13,028M → capex/sales = 0.10x

This is very telling:
- Connectivity's capex-to-sales ratio has been steadily declining: 0.63x → 0.46x → 0.37x → 0.10x, showing a maturing business where infrastructure investment is growing more slowly than revenue.
- AI's ratio skyrocketed and then came down somewhat in Q1 2026 (but Q1 is just one quarter, could be lumpy).

Let me now also look at what else could be relevant. Let me check for any MD&A discussion about AI infrastructure build-out and Connectivity maturity.

arguments.prompt: What does the MD&A say about AI infrastructure build-out, data center investments, and the phase of AI capex relative to revenue? How does management characterize the AI segment's growth phase? {{spacex_s1}}
What does the MD&A say about Connectivity segment maturity, Starlink growth, and how capex trends relate to revenue growth? {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
The MD&A shows the AI segment is in a heavy infrastructure build‑out phase, with capital expenditures dramatically outpacing revenue. Management characterizes the segment as an early‑stage, high‑investment business prioritizing growth over near‑term profitability.

---

### 1. AI Infrastructure Build‑out and Data Center Investments

**Liquidity and Capital Resources section**  
The year‑over‑year increase in net cash used in investing activities is *“primarily driven by an increase in capital expenditures of $6,748 million related to the build out of data centers and related infrastructure.”* Financing cash flows surged largely due to *“an increase in proceeds from debt and other financing arrangements for our AI segment of $16,055 million”*—funding raised specifically to support that infrastructure build‑out (via the SpaceX Bridge Loan and equity sales).

**Capital expenditure detail by segment**  
AI‑specific capex figures show a rapid, multi‑year acceleration:

| Period | AI Capex | 
|--------|----------|
| Q1’26 (unaudited) | $7,723 million |
| Q1’25 (unaudited) | $2,567 million |
| FY2025 | $12,727 million |
| FY2024 | $5,633 million |
| FY2023 | $463 million |

The MD&A explains the Q1’26 increase was *“primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure.”* This language signals that data center construction—not just equipment purchases—is the core use of capital.

**Balance sheet note**  
Construction‑in‑progress, the largest PP&E category, is *“primarily comprised of ongoing construction and expansion of the facilities and equipment as well as AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service.”* This confirms a massive pipeline of un‑depreciated assets yet to come online.

**Strategic rationale**  
The preamble underscores why this build‑out is occurring: *“Frontier AI has become fundamentally infrastructure‑constrained. … Compute infrastructure scale helps determine model iteration speed, model quality, and capital efficiency—making infrastructure itself a critical capability.”* Management is betting that owning massive GPU clusters and purpose‑built data centers will be a competitive moat.

---

### 2. AI Capex Relative to Revenue

The ratio of capex to revenue highlights how early the investment cycle is:

| Metric | Q1’26 | FY2025 | FY2024 |
|--------|-------|--------|--------|
| AI segment revenue | $818 M | $3,201 M | N/A (segment not separated) |
| AI segment capex | $7,723 M | $12,727 M | $5,633 M |
| Capex / Revenue | ~9.4x | ~4.0x | N/A |

Revenue is a fraction of the capital being deployed. The AI segment remains deeply unprofitable: for Q1’26, it reported a loss from operations of $(2,469) million and Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $(609) million. For FY2025, it lost $(6,355) million from operations with $(1,237) million in Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The MD&A directly ties these losses to the build‑out: *“AI loss from operations … increased … primarily driven by higher cloud computing and GPU depreciation costs, data center infrastructure and employee expenses, partially offset by higher revenue.”*

The **Risk Factors** section explicitly frames the timeline: *“we plan to allocate substantial capital to build our AI compute infrastructure, and we expect a multi‑year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA.”* This is not a short‑term inflection; management is projecting years of heavy capex before the segment reaches sustained profitability.

---

### 3. Management’s Characterization of the AI Segment’s Growth Phase

Management uses clear language to describe the AI segment’s stage:

* **“Earlier stage of development”** – The segment overview states that the FY2025 losses reflect its *“earlier stage of development and continued investments to support long‑term growth opportunities in AI.”*
* **Growth‑over‑profit posture** – *“In our newly acquired AI segment, we plan to prioritize growth and investment to capture significant opportunities in AI applications and compute infrastructure.”*
* **Rapid scaling narrative** – The SG&A discussion notes higher employee costs *“as our AI business grew rapidly”* and R&D expenses are driven by *“depreciation of GPU hardware and the cost of cloud computing as a result of our AI data center expansions.”*
* **Future operating leverage expectation** – While AI cost of revenue will rise in absolute dollars, management expects it *“to decrease as a percentage of revenue as we monetize our products and as we expand our service offerings.”* This signals that the current high‑cost structure is a temporary feature of the investment phase.
* **Persistent losses acknowledged** – The Risk Factors warn that the AI segment *“has incurred significant operating losses since inception, and we may not achieve profitability in this segment, or, if achieved, sustain it.”*

---

### Summary

The MD&A paints a picture of an AI segment in the midst of an unprecedented infrastructure build‑out. Data center construction and GPU deployments are the primary drivers of investing cash flows, financed by large‑scale debt and equity raises. Capex dwarfs revenue by multiples, and operating losses are widening as depreciation and facility costs mount. Management portrays this as the natural “earlier stage” of a long‑term growth bet, with a multi‑year runway before the segment can generate sustained positive Adjusted EBITDA. The emphasis throughout is on capturing a massive AI opportunity, even at the expense of near‑term profitability.

Based on the provided MD&A excerpts, here is the analysis of the Connectivity segment's maturity, Starlink growth, and the relationship between capex trends and revenue growth:

*   **Connectivity Segment Maturity**: The Connectivity segment is described as a maturing, rapidly scaling business that is driving operational efficiencies. Management explicitly expects cost of revenue to "decrease as a percentage of revenue as we continue to drive efficiencies," and for R&D expenses to also "decrease as a percentage of revenue as we scale our business." The segment’s strong performance is attributed to "continued improvement in network efficiency."
*   **Starlink Growth**: Starlink is experiencing hyper-growth in subscribers, but this is coupled with a declining average revenue per user (ARPU).
    *   **Subscriber Growth**: The subscriber base grew by 104.7% in Q1 2026 and 99.9% for the full year 2025.
    *   **ARPU Decline**: This subscriber growth was "offset by a 22.9% decline in Starlink Subscriber ARPU" in Q1 2026, a trend attributed to "international expansion and the addition of lower priced service plans."
    *   **Revenue Growth**: Overall revenue from consumer and enterprise customers continues to increase significantly, with enterprise growth being driven by aviation, maritime, and mobile connectivity businesses.
*   **Capex Trends and Revenue Growth**: The provided MD&A excerpts **do not explicitly discuss the relationship between capital expenditure (capex) trends and revenue growth** for the Connectivity segment.
    *   The analysis of cost increases focuses on the *consequences* of past capex (e.g., "higher depreciation of capitalized launch and satellite costs due to the increase in Starlink flights") and rising operating expenses for international expansion, rather than a forward-looking discussion of planned investment levels relative to revenue.
    *   The segment's narrative centers on growing operational leverage, with forward-looking expectations focused on reducing the cost of revenue, R&D, and SG&A as a percentage of revenue over time, without linking this to future capex spending levels.

steps.message: Now let me compute the exact multiples
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compute the key metrics carefully.

**AI Segment (Q1 2026):**
- Capex: $7,723 million
- Revenue: $818 million
- Annualized revenue: $818 × 4 = $3,272 million
- Implied capex-to-sales (annualized): $7,723 / $3,272 = 2.3604... ≈ 2.36x

**Connectivity Segment (Q1 2026):**
- Capex: $1,332 million
- Revenue: $3,257 million
- Annualized revenue: $3,257 × 4 = $13,028 million
- Implied capex-to-sales (annualized): $1,332 / $13,028 = 0.1022... ≈ 0.10x

The disparity is enormous: AI's capex-to-sales is about 23x higher than Connectivity's.

Now let me also look at historical trends:

AI capex-to-sales (using annual revenue, not annualized):
- FY2023: $463M / $2,961M = 0.156x
- FY2024: $5,633M / $2,620M = 2.15x
- FY2025: $12,727M / $3,201M = 3.98x
- Q1 2026 (annualized): $7,723M / $3,272M = 2.36x

Connectivity capex-to-sales:
- FY2023: $2,455M / $3,869M = 0.63x
- FY2024: $3,498M / $7,599M = 0.46x
- FY2025: $4,178M / $11,387M = 0.37x
- Q1 2026 (annualized): $1,332M / $13,028M = 0.10x

Let me also compute the operating margins:
- AI Q1 2026: -$2,469M / $818M = -301.8%
- Connectivity Q1 2026: $1,188M / $3,257M = 36.5%

This is a very clean story. Let me present the analysis.

Let me also compute the "capex intensity" differently - capex as % of revenue on a quarterly basis:
- AI Q1 2026: 7,723 / 818 = 944% (approx 9.4x quarterly)
- Connectivity Q1 2026: 1,332 / 3,257 = 40.9%

Let me compute the exact numbers:

AI annualized revenue: 818 * 4 = 3272
AI capex-to-sales: 7723 / 3272 = 2.3604...

Connectivity annualized revenue: 3257 * 4 = 13028
Connectivity capex-to-sales: 1332 / 13028 = 0.10224...

Now, let me think about what the disparity implies.

The AI segment is spending $2.36 on capex for every $1 of annualized revenue. This is characteristic of an early-stage infrastructure build phase where:
1. Massive data centers need to be constructed before they can generate revenue
2. GPU clusters must be purchased and deployed before AI services can be offered
3. Depreciation from these assets will flow through the P&L over time
4. Revenue is still small relative to the asset base being built

The Connectivity segment is spending only $0.10 on capex for every $1 of annualized revenue. This indicates:
1. The Starlink constellation is already substantially built (9,600+ satellites)
2. Revenue is now being harvested from the existing infrastructure
3. The business is generating strong operating profits ($1,188M in Q1)
4. The infrastructure is largely in place and incremental capex is modest relative to the revenue base

The connectivity segment's trajectory is instructive: it went from 0.63x in FY2023 to 0.10x in Q1 2026 annualized - showing the classic infrastructure-to-maturity arc. The AI segment may follow a similar path, but it's much earlier in that cycle.

Let me now write this up and submit.
Now let me also compute some additional ratios for more depth. Let me also compute the historical trends more precisely.
Now I have all the numbers. Let me also compute the AI Q1 2025 capex-to-sales for comparison.

**AI Segment:**
| Period | Capex ($M) | Revenue ($M) | Capex/Revenue (quarterly) | Capex/Annualized Revenue |
|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|
| FY2023 | 463 | 2,961 | N/A | 0.16x |

arguments.prompt: What does the S-1 say about the capital intensity and investment cycle of the Connectivity segment historically? How did Connectivity's capex trend evolve from its early build-out phase to its current more mature state? {{spacex_s1}}
What is the total revenue, operating income, and Adjusted EBITDA for the consolidated company for Q1 2026? Also provide segment-level Adjusted EBITDA if available. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
The S-1 describes the Connectivity segment’s capital intensity and investment cycle as evolving from an early, externally funded build‑out phase to a more mature, self‑sustaining stage—while still investing meaningfully in network expansion.  

**Early build‑out phase (historical funding model)**  
- The company “invested in our Connectivity segment as we generated Segment Adjusted EBITDA from our Space segment, along with additional equity capital that we raised externally.”  
- This capital funded the initial deployment of the Starlink satellite constellation, creating a segment that now generates “predictable and recurring revenue.”  

**Transition to maturity and sustained profitability**  
- The Connectivity segment “became in aggregate Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive on a sustained basis beginning in 2023,” marking a key inflection point.  
- By 2025, the segment generated **$4,423 million** in income from operations and **$7,168 million** in Segment Adjusted EBITDA, with revenue of **$11,387 million** (up 49.8% YoY). This demonstrates that the business is now highly profitable and capable of self‑funding further investments.

**How capex trend evolved (capital intensity)**  
Absolute capital expenditure continued to increase each year, but the pace of growth relative to revenue moderated, reflecting declining capital intensity as the network scaled:

| Year | Connectivity Capex | YoY Change | Revenue (for reference) | Capex as % of Revenue |
|------|-------------------|------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| 2023 | $2,455 M          | –          | Not separately stated*   | –                      |
| 2024 | $3,498 M          | +$1,043 M  | $7,599 M                 | ~46%                   |
| 2025 | $4,178 M          | +$680 M    | $11,387 M                | ~37%                   |
| Q1 2025 | $814 M        | –          | –                        | –                      |
| Q1 2026 | $1,332 M      | +$518 M    | –                        | –                      |

*Source: Capex data from MD&A; 2024 revenue from segment table; 2025 revenue from Preamble.*

**Key takeaways from the S‑1**  
- The year‑over‑year increases in capex were “primarily driven by higher satellite and ground equipment costs as we continue to increase our number of satellites and grow our satellite network.”  
- Even as capex rose, revenue grew much faster, causing **capex as a percentage of revenue to fall**—from about 46% in 2024 to 37% in 2025—indicating that the network’s capital intensity is declining in its more mature state.  
- The company acknowledges that it will “continue to invest meaningfully” in Starlink’s infrastructure, but today those investments are supported by strong internal cash flows rather than the heavy reliance on external capital that characterized the early build‑out.  

In summary, the S-1 portrays the Connectivity capex trend as moving from a high‑intensity, externally funded deployment phase (pre‑2023) to a mature phase where the segment is solidly EBITDA‑positive, revenue is scaling rapidly, and capex—though still growing—represents a shrinking share of revenue, reflecting a more efficient, self‑reinforcing investment cycle.

Based on the provided financial data, here are the figures for the consolidated company and its segments for Q1 2026:

*   **Total Revenue**: $4,694 million
*   **Operating Income (Loss)**: $(1,943) million
*   **Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA**: $1,127 million

**Segment-Level Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026:**
*   **Space**: $(351) million
*   **Connectivity**: $2,087 million
*   **AI**: $(609) million

Q1 2026:

- AI capex-to-sales (annualized): $7,723 / $3,272 = 2.36x

- Connectivity capex: $1,332M
- Connectivity revenue: $3,257M

Now let me write the final answer. I'll structure it as:

2. Calculate Connectivity capex-to-sales multiple
3. Compare the two
4. Interpret the disparity in terms of maturity vs infrastructure-build phase economics
5. Support with historical trends showing Connectivity's own arc from build-out to maturity

Let me also note: the question says "implied capex-to-sales multiple." This could also be interpreted as the ratio of annualized capex to annualized revenue. Let me stick with Q1 capex / annualized Q1 revenue since the question says "Using Q1 2026 AI capex and annualized AI revenue."

Let me now draft the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Implied Capex-to-Sales Multiples: AI vs. Connectivity (Q1 2026)

### Calculation

| Metric | AI Segment | Connectivity Segment |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 Capital Expenditures | $7,723M | $1,332M |
| Q1 2026 Revenue | $818M | $3,257M |
| Annualized Revenue (×4) | $3,272M | $13,028M |
| **Implied Capex-to-Sales (Annualized)** | **2.36×** | **0.10×** |
| Q1 2026 Operating Income (Loss) | $(2,469)M | $1,188M |
| Q1 2026 Operating Margin | −301.8% | +36.5% |
| Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $(609)M | $2,087M |

---

### The Disparity

AI spends **$2.36 on capex for every dollar of annualized revenue** — roughly **23× the capital intensity** of Connectivity, which spends only $0.10 per dollar of annualized revenue. This is not a marginal difference; it reflects fundamentally different economic phases.

---

### What the Disparity Implies

**AI: Infrastructure-Build Phase**

The AI segment is in a heavy front-loaded infrastructure deployment cycle. Q1 2026 capex of $7.7 billion — nearly 9.4× the quarter's revenue — is directed at "the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure." The S-1 explicitly frames this as a multi-year investment horizon: management states it expects "a multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

Key markers of the build phase:
- Capex is growing exponentially: $463M (FY2023) → $5,633M (FY2024) → $12,727M (FY2025), with Q1 2026 alone nearly matching FY2024's full-year spend.
- Revenue, while present, is a byproduct of a platform still being constructed. The $818M in Q1 2026 revenue is dwarfed by $2,379M in R&D and $1,493M in D&A alone.
- Operating losses are deep and widening: $(2,469)M in Q1 2026 vs. $(936)M in Q1 2025, driven by GPU depreciation, cloud computing costs, and data center operating expenses that precede revenue generation.
- The balance-sheet note confirms a large pipeline of "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service" (construction-in-progress), meaning the capex being deployed today has not even begun depreciating — let alone generating revenue.

**Connectivity: Maturing Harvest Phase**

Connectivity's 0.10× capex-to-sales ratio signals a business that has largely built its asset base and is now harvesting revenue from it. With over 9,600 Starlink satellites already deployed, incremental capex is modest relative to a large and growing revenue stream.

Key markers of maturity:
- The ratio has declined steadily: 0.63× (FY2023) → 0.46× (FY2024) → 0.37× (FY2025) → 0.10× (Q1 2026 annualized). This is the signature of a network business moving down the cost curve.
- Operating leverage is pronounced: Connectivity generated $1,188M of operating income (36.5% margin) in Q1 2026. It became "Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive on a sustained basis beginning in 2023."
- Revenue is scaling faster than capex: FY2025 revenue grew 49.8% YoY to $11,387M while capex grew only ~19% to $4,178M.
- The business is self-funding: strong internal cash flows now support ongoing investment, a sharp contrast to AI's reliance on $16+ billion in external debt and equity financing.

---

### Connectivity as the Template: The Build-to-Harvest Arc

Connectivity's own history provides the most instructive lens on AI's trajectory. In FY2023, Connectivity consumed $0.63 in capex per dollar of revenue — far below AI's current 2.36×, but still 6× higher than its current 0.10× ratio. The segment transitioned from an externally funded deployment phase (pre-2023) through a scaling phase (2023–2024) to its current mature, cash-generative state. The S-1 notes that the early Connectivity build-out was funded by Space segment EBITDA and external equity — structurally analogous to how AI is being funded today.

AI today is earlier and more capital-intensive than Connectivity ever was (GPU clusters and purpose-built data centers require far more upfront capital per unit of eventual revenue than satellites), but the economic arc — massive upfront deployment, followed by a declining capex-to-sales ratio as revenue scales into the asset base — is directionally the same.

---

### Key Caveats

1. **All figures reflect the recast consolidated entity** incorporating xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025). No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations.
2. **Q1 2026 is a single quarter** — capex can be lumpy, and the annualized revenue approach assumes Q1 revenue is representative of the full year. If AI revenue is back-half weighted (plausible given data-center commissioning schedules), the true annual capex-to-sales ratio may be lower than 2.36×.
3. **The AI segment's capex-to-sales ratio already shows signs of peaking**: FY2025 was 3.98× on full-year figures, and Q1 2026 annualized at 2.36× suggests the ratio may be beginning to decline as revenue growth begins to partially catch up with the infrastructure deployment.

---

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

| Q1 2026 Revenue | $818M | $3,257M |
| Annualized Revenue (×4) | $3,272M | $13,028M |
| **Implied Capex-to-Sales (Annualized)** | **2.36×** | **0.10×** |
| Q1 2026 Operating Income (Loss) | $(2,469)M | $1,188M |
| Q1 2026 Operating Margin | −301.8% | +36.5% |
| Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $(609)M | $2,087M |

---

### The Disparity

AI spends **$2.36 on capex for every dollar of annualized revenue** — roughly **23× the capital intensity** of Connectivity, which spends only $0.10 per dollar of annualized revenue. This is not a marginal difference; it reflects fundamentally different economic phases.

---

### What the Disparity Implies

**AI: Infrastructure-Build Phase**

The AI segment is in a heavy front-loaded infrastructure deployment cycle. Q1 2026 capex of $7.7 billion — nearly 9.4× the quarter's revenue — is directed at "the rapid expansion of terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure." The S-1 explicitly frames this as a multi-year investment horizon: management states it expects "a multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

Key markers of the build phase:
- Capex is growing exponentially: $463M (FY2023) → $5,633M (FY2024) → $12,727M (FY2025), with Q1 2026 alone nearly matching FY2024's full-year spend.
- Revenue, while present, is a byproduct of a platform still being constructed. The $818M in Q1 2026 revenue is dwarfed by $2,379M in R&D and $1,493M in D&A alone.
- Operating losses are deep and widening: $(2,469)M in Q1 2026 vs. $(936)M in Q1 2025, driven by GPU depreciation, cloud computing costs, and data center operating expenses that precede revenue generation.
- The balance-sheet note confirms a large pipeline of "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service" (construction-in-progress), meaning the capex being deployed today has not even begun depreciating — let alone generating revenue.

**Connectivity: Maturing Harvest Phase**

Connectivity's 0.10× capex-to-sales ratio signals a business that has largely built its asset base and is now harvesting revenue from it. With over 9,600 Starlink satellites already deployed, incremental capex is modest relative to a large and growing revenue stream.

Key markers of maturity:
- The ratio has declined steadily: 0.63× (FY2023) → 0.46× (FY2024) → 0.37× (FY2025) → 0.10× (Q1 2026 annualized). This is the signature of a network business moving down the cost curve.
- Operating leverage is pronounced: Connectivity generated $1,188M of operating income (36.5% margin) in Q1 2026. It became "Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive on a sustained basis beginning in 2023."
- Revenue is scaling faster than capex: FY2025 revenue grew 49.8% YoY to $11,387M while capex grew only ~19% to $4,178M.
- The business is self-funding: strong internal cash flows now support ongoing investment, a sharp contrast to AI's reliance on $16+ billion in external debt and equity financing.

---

### Connectivity as the Template: The Build-to-Harvest Arc

Connectivity's own history provides the most instructive lens on AI's trajectory. In FY2023, Connectivity consumed $0.63 in capex per dollar of revenue — far below AI's current 2.36×, but still 6× higher than its current 0.10× ratio. The segment transitioned from an externally funded deployment phase (pre-2023) through a scaling phase (2023–2024) to its current mature, cash-generative state. The S-1 notes that the early Connectivity build-out was funded by Space segment EBITDA and external equity — structurally analogous to how AI is being funded today.

AI today is earlier and more capital-intensive than Connectivity ever was (GPU clusters and purpose-built data centers require far more upfront capital per unit of eventual revenue than satellites), but the economic arc — massive upfront deployment, followed by a declining capex-to-sales ratio as revenue scales into the asset base — is directionally the same.

---

### Key Caveats

1. **All figures reflect the recast consolidated entity** incorporating xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025). No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations.
2. **Q1 2026 is a single quarter** — capex can be lumpy, and the annualized revenue approach assumes Q1 revenue is representative of the full year. If AI revenue is back-half weighted (plausible given data-center commissioning schedules), the true annual capex-to-sales ratio may be lower than 2.36×.
3. **The AI segment's capex-to-sales ratio already shows signs of peaking**: FY2025 was 3.98× on full-year figures, and Q1 2026 annualized at 2.36× suggests the ratio may be beginning to decline as revenue growth begins to partially catch up with the infrastructure deployment.
